African Population Genomics: Structure, Diversity, and Pharmacogenomic Relevance

PCA · ADMIXTURE · Weir–Cockerham Fst · CYP2D6/CYP2D7 Regional Analysis across 2,504 individuals, 26 populations, chromosome 22 (GRCh38)

Dr Salihu Timothy, M.B.Ch.B.
Bioinformatics Portfolio Project
1000 Genomes Project Phase 3 · GRCh38
github.com/DrTim105/african-population-genomics
Data: 1000 Genomes Phase 3 (2022 high-coverage) PLINK2 v2.0.0a.6.9 ADMIXTURE 1.3.0 R 4.5.3 · tidyverse Chromosome 22 · 5,141 LD-pruned SNPs Assembly: GRCh38/hg38 Fst method: Weir & Cockerham (1984)
Background & Motivation

African populations harbour more genetic diversity than any other continental group — a direct consequence of humanity's African origin and the serial founder effects accompanying out-of-Africa migrations. Yet they remain underrepresented in both genomic research databases and pharmacogenomic dosing guidelines.


Clinical Relevance

CYP2D6 metabolises ~25% of all commonly prescribed drugs. The African-specific allele CYP2D6*17 (present in 20–34% of West and East Africans) reduces enzyme activity — yet standard dosing guidelines do not account for it. This project tests whether population-level structure in commonly-used genotype panels reaches into the CYP2D6/CYP2D7 cluster.


Data
2,504 Unrelated samples (Phase 3 panel)
26 Populations across 5 superpopulations
5,141 LD-pruned SNPs after QC
661 AFR samples across 7 populations

Methods Pipeline
1
QC Filter
PLINK2 --maf 0.05 --geno 0.02
102,467 biallelic SNPs retained
↓
2
LD Pruning
--indep-pairwise 50 10 0.1
5,141 independent SNPs; avg spacing 9.9kb
↓
3
Global PCA
PLINK2 --pca 10
Top 10 principal components
↓
4
Ancestry Inference
ADMIXTURE --cv K=2–6
K=5 selected via CV error elbow
↓
5
Pairwise Fst
PLINK2 --fst method=wc
10 global + 21 within-Africa pairs
↓
6
Regional Analysis
R + permutation test
CYP2D6/CYP2D7 50kb window; n=1,000 permutations

Study Populations

AFR: YRI · ESN · GWD · MSL · LWK · ACB · ASW
EUR: CEU · GBR · FIN · IBS · TSI
EAS: CHB · JPT · CHS · CDX · KHV
SAS: GIH · PJL · BEB · STU · ITU
AMR: MXL · PUR · CLM · PEL

Global Population Structure

Fig 1. PCA of 5,141 LD-pruned chromosome 22 SNPs (n=2,504). PC1 (53.3%) separates AFR from all non-African populations. PC2 (21.6%) separates East Asian from European/South Asian clusters. AMR individuals scatter along an admixture gradient between AFR and EUR.

Key Finding 1

PC1 (53.3%) is the AFR vs. non-AFR axis — the largest single source of human genetic variation on chr22, reflecting the out-of-Africa bottleneck.

Unsupervised Ancestry Inference (ADMIXTURE K=5)

Fig 2. ADMIXTURE ancestry proportions at K=5, sorted by superpopulation. Each vertical bar = one individual; colors = inferred ancestry components. K=5 selected via cross-validation error elbow (K5→K6 drop is 6× smaller than K4→K5).

Key Finding 2

Without seeing any population labels, ADMIXTURE independently recovers 5 components that map 1:1 onto the 5 continental superpopulations. AFR is the most heterogeneous; EAS and EUR are most homogeneous.

Global Fst: Pairwise Superpopulation Differentiation

Fig 3. Mean Weir–Cockerham Fst for all 10 superpopulation pairs across 5,141 chr22 variants. AFR–EAS is the most differentiated pair (not AFR–EUR), consistent with the serial founder effect model (Ramachandran et al. 2005). AMR–EUR is lowest, reflecting recent post-colonial admixture.

Key Finding 3 — Unexpected Result

AFR–EAS (Fst ≈ 0.12) is more differentiated than AFR–EUR (Fst ≈ 0.10). East Asian populations passed through more sequential bottlenecks during migration, accumulating greater differentiation from the African source.

Centering Africa: Within-Population Structure

Fig 4. Within-Africa PCA restricted to 5 continental populations (ACB/ASW excluded to isolate geographic structure). LWK (Luhya, Kenya) separates from West African cluster on PC1 (13.3%) — the ~5,500km West–East geographic split.

Key Finding 4

When diaspora admixture is removed, genuine geographic structure within Africa becomes visible: West Africa vs. East Africa on PC1.

Fig 5. Mean pairwise Fst across all 21 pairs of 7 AFR populations. ESN–YRI (both Nigerian) is lowest (Fst=0.001). Maximum within-Africa Fst (~0.015) is 6–7× smaller than AFR–EUR (0.098) — directly demonstrating Lewontin (1972): most human variation exists within, not between, populations.

Geographic Context

Fig 6. AFR sampling locations colored by mean Fst from YRI. Diaspora populations (ASW, ACB) show higher Fst from YRI than LWK (Kenya, 5,500km away). Admixture creates more genetic distance than geography.

Pharmacogenomic Signal: CYP2D6 / CYP2D7

Fig 7. AFR–EUR Fst across the CYP2D6/CYP2D7 gene cluster. Top hit (Fst=0.461, 4.7× genome-wide mean) confirmed inside CYP2D7 via UCSC GRCh38. Window mean Fst=0.1353 vs genome-wide 0.0977 (p=0.168; underpowered at n=11 SNPs due to MAF+LD pruning removing functional variants).

Key Finding 5 — Clinical Signal

The strongest regional signal maps to CYP2D7, the pseudogene that generates CYP2D6 hybrid alleles through gene conversion — directly connected to population differences in drug metabolism.

Conclusions
  • Five continental superpopulations are cleanly resolved by both PCA and ADMIXTURE using chromosome 22 alone
  • AFR–EAS, not AFR–EUR, is the most differentiated pair — consistent with serial founder effects
  • Within-Africa max Fst (~0.015) is 6–7× smaller than AFR–EUR, replicating Lewontin (1972)
  • Diaspora admixture creates more genetic distance than 5,500km of geographic separation within Africa
  • The CYP2D6/CYP2D7 region shows elevated Fst in this common-variant panel, though full pharmacogenomics analysis requires a dedicated pipeline retaining rare alleles

Selected References

Byrska-Bishop M et al. (2022) High-coverage whole-genome sequencing of the expanded 1000 Genomes Project cohort. Cell 185:3426–3440 · Weir BS & Cockerham CC (1984) Estimating F-statistics for the analysis of population structure. Evolution 38:1358 · Lewontin RC (1972) The apportionment of human diversity. Evol Biol 6:381 · Ramachandran S et al. (2005) Support from the relationship of genetic and geographic distance. PNAS 102:15942 · Gaedigk A et al. (2018) The PharmVar Consortium. Clin Pharmacol Ther 103:399
